diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Profuse watery diarrhoea after gluten exposure is characteristic of an autoimmune enteropathy flare.
High-volume fluid loss is the mechanism of the dehydration that defines the disposition.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset within hours of accidental gluten exposure, matching prior flares; no fever or sick contacts

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear temporal gluten trigger with a stereotyped prior pattern supports a coeliac flare.
Immediate post-gluten onset without fever or sick contacts argues against an infective cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Periumbilical crampy abdominal pain, proportionate to the diarrhoea

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse crampy periumbilical pain fits enteropathic cramping rather than a localised surgical process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inability to tolerate oral fluids with orthostatic presyncope and reduced urine output

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Presyncope with failed oral intake and oliguria indicates significant volume depletion requiring IV rehydration.

symptoms.symptom_name: No blood per rectum or haematemesis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of visible blood in stool or vomit lowers the probability of a significant GI bleed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse proportionate pain without localisation, rigidity, or rebound

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse crampy proportionate pain without peritonism argues against a surgical abdomen; definitive exclusion needs examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia with delayed capillary refill (3s), dry mucous membranes, sluggish skin turgor; soft non-distended abdomen without guarding/rebound (in-person examination findings)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Delayed capillary refill, dry mucosae, and sluggish turgor objectively confirm significant dehydration. Clinician-only findings sealed from the AI-Doctor-readable layer.
A soft, non-distended abdomen without guarding or rebound argues strongly against peritonitis; not assessable via telehealth.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Failed oral intake with presyncope plus objective signs of poor perfusion define clinically significant dehydration requiring IV rehydration in person.
No bleeding and diffuse proportionate pain without peritonism together lower the probability of a surgical/bleeding emergency, supporting a dehydration-focused pathway with safety-netting.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person clinical assessment with hydration status and abdominal examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms dehydration severity, excludes a surgical abdomen, and enables IV rehydration that telehealth cannot deliver.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: IV rehydration and point-of-care electrolytes/renal function
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Needed to correct volume depletion and detect electrolyte disturbance/AKI when oral rehydration has failed.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine coeliac micronutrient panel (iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, zinc, magnesium)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Appropriate for outpatient follow-up of nutritional status; not required acutely and should not delay rehydration.
No evidence of bacterial infection; antibiotics are not indicated for a gluten-triggered coeliac flare.
